---
title: "3b Activity: Code-Generated Results"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    link-external-newwindow: true
    css: _materials.css
    include-after-body: _progress.html
execute:
  enabled: false
---


## Goal

We will add a few analysis tools to the practice project, update `paper.qmd` so it generates results from code, and render the paper again.

Note: The checkboxes on this page are only stored locally in your browser. They are not submitted or synced anywhere.

::: {.guide-progress}

Progress: <span data-progress-count>0</span> / <span data-progress-total>0</span> steps complete.

<button class="reset-progress" type="button" data-progress-reset>Reset progress</button>

:::


## Activity

::: {.panel-tabset}

## 1. Add Analysis Packages

Open the `python_tmr_practice` repository in Codespaces.

Open `pyproject.toml` and add three packages to the `dependencies` list:

```toml
"maketables==0.1.8",
"matplotlib==3.10.9",
"statsmodels==0.14.6",
```

Install from the project:

```bash
python -m pip install -e .
```

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-add-packages">
  <span>I added the pinned analysis packages to `pyproject.toml`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-install-packages">
  <span>I installed from the updated project file.</span>
</label>

:::


## 2. Add Results Boilerplate

Open `paper.qmd`.

Find the section named `## A Small Code Example`.

Replace that section, stopping before `## References`, with this:

<p class="copy-note">Click inside the block to select it, then copy and paste it into `paper.qmd`.</p>

<textarea class="copy-source" readonly spellcheck="false" rows="44">
## Data and Results

&#96;&#96;&#96;{python}
#| label: setup-results
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

from random import Random

import maketables as mt
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from pandas.plotting import scatter_matrix


rng = Random(2026)

rows = []
for firm_id in range(1, 121):
    size = rng.gauss(0, 1)
    attention = rng.gauss(0, 1)
    slack = 0.35 * size + rng.gauss(0, 0.8)
    performance = (
        0.45 * attention
        + 0.25 * slack
        + 0.20 * size
        + rng.gauss(0, 1)
    )

    rows.append(
        {
            "firm_id": firm_id,
            "attention": attention,
            "slack": slack,
            "size": size,
            "performance": performance,
        }
    )

results_data = pd.DataFrame(rows)
analysis_vars = ["performance", "attention", "slack", "size"]
variable_labels = {
    "performance": "Performance",
    "attention": "Attention",
    "slack": "Slack",
    "size": "Size",
    "Intercept": "Constant",
}

descriptives_table = mt.DTable(
    results_data,
    vars=analysis_vars,
    stats=["count", "mean", "std", "min", "max"],
    labels=variable_labels,
    stats_labels={"count": "N", "std": "SD"},
)

correlations = results_data[analysis_vars].corr().round(2)
for row_number in range(len(correlations)):
    for column_number in range(row_number + 1, len(correlations.columns)):
        correlations.iat[row_number, column_number] = pd.NA
correlations = (
    correlations.astype(object)
    .where(pd.notna(correlations), "")
)
correlations.index = [variable_labels[name] for name in correlations.index]
correlations.columns = [variable_labels[name] for name in correlations.columns]
correlation_table = mt.MTable(correlations)

model = smf.ols(
    "performance ~ attention + slack + size",
    data=results_data,
).fit()

regression_table = mt.ETable(
    [model],
    labels=variable_labels,
    model_stats=["N", "r2", "adj_r2"],
    coef_fmt="b:.3f* \n (se:.3f)",
    signif_code=[0.001, 0.01, 0.05],
)

regression_display = regression_table.df.copy()
regression_display.index = regression_display.index.get_level_values(-1)
regression_display.columns = regression_display.columns.get_level_values(0)
regression_display = regression_display.rename_axis("Variable").reset_index()

sample_size = len(results_data)
attention_coef = model.params["attention"]
attention_p = model.pvalues["attention"]
attention_coef_text = f"{attention_coef:.2f}"
attention_p_text = "&lt; .001" if attention_p &lt; 0.001 else f"{attention_p:.3f}"
&#96;&#96;&#96;

The sample includes `{python} sample_size` firm-year observations.

### Descriptive Statistics

&#96;&#96;&#96;{python}
#| label: tbl-descriptives
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive statistics for simulated firm-year data."
#| output: asis
#| echo: false

print(descriptives_table.df.to_markdown(floatfmt=".2f"))
&#96;&#96;&#96;

### Correlations

&#96;&#96;&#96;{python}
#| label: tbl-correlations
#| tbl-cap: "Correlations among simulated analysis variables."
#| output: asis
#| echo: false

print(correlation_table.df.to_markdown())
&#96;&#96;&#96;

### Scatter Matrix

&#96;&#96;&#96;{python}
#| label: fig-scatter-matrix
#| fig-cap: "Scatter matrix for simulated analysis variables."
#| fig-width: 5.5
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| out-width: "85%"
#| echo: false

axes = scatter_matrix(
    results_data[analysis_vars],
    figsize=(5.5, 5.5),
    diagonal="kde",
    alpha=0.7,
)
fig = axes[0, 0].figure
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
&#96;&#96;&#96;

### Regression Results

Attention is associated with performance in the simulated data
(b = `{python} attention_coef_text`, p = `{python} attention_p_text`).

&#96;&#96;&#96;{python}
#| label: tbl-regression
#| tbl-cap: "Regression predicting simulated performance."
#| output: asis
#| echo: false

print(regression_display.to_markdown(index=False))
&#96;&#96;&#96;

Standard errors are in parentheses.
Significance levels: * p < .05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001.
</textarea>

Save the file.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-replace-code-example">
  <span>I replaced the small code example with the generated-results section.</span>
</label>

:::


## 3. Render The Paper

In the terminal, render the paper:

```bash
quarto render paper.qmd
```

Then render the whole project:

```bash
quarto render
```

Open the rendered output and look for:

- the inline sample size;
- the descriptive statistics table;
- the lower-triangle correlation table;
- the scatter matrix;
- the regression table with model diagnostics;
- the inline regression coefficient and p value.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-render-paper">
  <span>I rendered `paper.qmd`.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-check-generated-results">
  <span>I found the generated numbers, tables, figure, and regression result.</span>
</label>

:::


## 4. Try One Change

In the hidden setup chunk, find this line:

```python
0.45 * attention
```

Change `0.45` to `0.75`.

Render `paper.qmd` again.

Check whether the regression table and inline coefficient changed.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-change-coefficient">
  <span>I changed one data-generating value and rendered the paper again.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-see-updated-results">
  <span>I saw the generated results update.</span>
</label>

:::


## 5. Commit And Push

Use Source Control:

1. Review the changes.
1. Stage `pyproject.toml` and `paper.qmd`.
1. Commit with a message such as:

```text
Added code-generated paper results.
```

Push or sync the commit to GitHub.

::: {.task-list}

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-review-results-diff">
  <span>I reviewed the generated-results changes.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-commit-results">
  <span>I committed the updated project and paper files.</span>
</label>

<label class="task-check">
  <input type="checkbox" data-progress-id="3b-push-results">
  <span>I pushed or synced the commit to GitHub.</span>
</label>

:::

:::
